### Ivan Machado

Federal University of Bahia (UFBA)of Alagoas (UFAL)of Salvador, BrazilMaceió, [tassio.virginio@ufba.br](mailto:tassio.virginio@ufba.br)[marcio@ic.ufal.br](mailto:marcio@ic.ufal.br)[ivan.machado@ufba.br](mailto:ivan.machado@ufba.br)

2Using the DNosetool , we initiallyconducteda studyon the**ABSTRACT** tool’s accuracy, and subsequently performed an analysis of open-Objective:This study explores the quality of tests in Dart, the main source projects written in Dart, examining the distribution of testlanguagefor mobile application development with the Flutter frame- smells, which ones occur most frequently, their causes, andco-work. Methods:The study begins by using the DNose tool, used occurrences.This study provides an overview of the current qualityto detect14 typesof test smellsin codewrittenin the Dartlan- of projects developed in the Dart language, crucial information forguage. Next, we evaluate the tool’s precision, accuracy, recall, and mobile application development.F1-score. Using this tool, we conduct a detailed analysis of tests in As a result, we generated an extensive dataset with over 907,566open-source projects extracted from the language’s central reposi- occurrencesof test smells, identifying that 74% of the analyzed teststory.Results: The study starts with a dataset of 5,410 Dart-language exhibit at least one of these issues. This analysis provides a com-projects, from which we were able to clone 4,154 repositories after prehensive view of the current quality of projects written in Dart,processing. Based on the cloned projects, we generated a dataset offeringessential insights to improve the language’s ecosystem andcontaining907,566 occurrences of test smells. Through our analysis, providethe Dart community with a clear perspective on the qualitywe characterized the specific types of test smells most frequently level of projects developed with it.encounteredand identified their causes. We observed the presence of test smells in 74% of test files. Another noticeable characteristic **2 Background**among the analyzed projects was the scarcity of tests, with 1,873 projectshaving one or no tests, which led us to expand the numberIn this section, we present all the concepts used in the development of analyzed projects to a broader base. Conclusion: This researchof this paper and necessary for its understanding. makes a significant contribution by providing insights into the qual- ity of tests in projects from Dart’s official repository, as well as by**2.1 Test**Smells offering an open-source tool for detecting 14 types of test smells. Testsmells were defined in 2001 by Deursen et38al.] and[are now well-establishedas one of the main approaches to assess the quality **KEYWORDS**of software tests [,29,32,33,36]. Today, there is a comprehensive Tests,Test Smells, Dart, Flutterlist of test smells identified across different programming languages [ 13 ]. In addition to the smells already discussed in the literature, we identifieda new type specific to the Dart and JavaScript languages: “TestWithout Description". In the following section, we present the**1 Introduction** test smells detected by the DNose tool. Smartphonesare the primary means of accessing information, used for readingnews, managingbank accounts,performingtransac-2.1.1**Assertion Roulette (AR)**.Happens when a test contains tions, amongother activities 7[All these servicesare accessedmore than one undocumented “expect” statement. Multiple asser- through mobile applications. Currently, the leading cross-platformtion statements in a test without a descriptive message impact the 1mobile application development framework is Flutter[37].maintainability,readability, and comprehensibility. In thisstudy,wefocusonthequalityofsystemsdevelopedDetection: It is detected when more than one “expect” does not using the Dart programming language, specifically mobile systems.have a “reason”. As we can see in Listing1, in this example,we As mentioned, Dart is nowadays of utmost importance in mobilehave a test smell “Assertion Roulette” on line 4, as the “expect” on application cross-platformdevelopment,beingthemostwidelyline 2 includes the “reason”, while the one on line 3 refers to the used. This study examined the quality of tests conductedin Darttest’s own description. projects.To assess test quality, we utilized the concept of test smells, based on previous studies that employed test smells to evaluate the1**test("** 2**expect**(1quality of test code [29, 31, 32, 40]. respect); TestSmells are anti-patterns introduced in test code that affect 3**expect**(1 test quality. There are studies on test smells in the Java program-4**expect**(1 5})ming language [, 29 , 31,32,40], in Python [, 43 ], in JavaScript [ 10 , 15], among others. To assist in identifying test smells, several **Listing 1: Example of an Assertion Roulet in Dart** tools have been developed for some languages3 , 12[ , 15,24,40,43].

### Empty

### Test (ET)

### .

### is considered empty.

2.1.10**Sensitive Equality**(SE).It happenswhen “toString”is**3 Empirical** utilized withinatest.Insuchcases,thetestsassessobjectsbyThis empirical evaluation aims to investigate the accuracy of DNose invokingthe object’s default “toString()” and matching the resultingin detecting test smells in the Dart language. We designed the em- output with a predefinedstring. Modificationsto the “toString()”pirical study in four stages, as shown in Figure implementation can cause the test to fail.**tion , in which we randomly define the tests to be analyzed from** Detection: A line that invokes the “toString()” and you “expect”real projects;(ii) Oracle Definition function, as we can see an example in listing 10.instances of test DNose to collect instances of test smells; and1**test("** 2String;in which we analyze the collected data to investigate our objectives. 3**expect**( " Toconstruct the dataset, we randomly selected repositories from 4}) our dataset, **Listing 10: Example of an Sensitive Equality in Dart**repository for the Dart language. We identified the test cases and randomly selected 140 tests from them. We then followed the defi- 2.1.11**Sleepy Fixture (SF)**.Explicitly putting a thread on holdnition to manually detect test smells. can result in unpredictable outcomes, as the time it takes to process a task may vary between different devices. This test smell is intro- duced by developers when they need to pause the execution of a test for a set period(e.g., simulatingan external event) and then continue with the execution. Detection: A line that invokes the “sleep()” function, as we can see an example in listing 11.  1**test("** oneSecond 2**test("** 3()**async**=>**await**Future 1)  **Listing 11: Example of an Sleepy Fixture in Dart**

2.1.12**Test Without**Description(TWD).A newtestsmell arises when a test function lacks a description, making it harder to read and understand the test code. Detection: A test that does not have its description,as we can see an example in listing 12.  1**test(""** 2**test("**`` ,") 3test"`` ,**if**( true)  **Listing 12: Example of an Test Without Description in Dart**

2.1.13**Unknown Test (UT)**.When a test lacks an “expect”, “ver- ify()”, or “assertion()” check. Detection: A test that does not contain an assertion statement**3.1 Oracle** “expect”,as we can see an example in listing 13. To manually 1**test("**crossed design to assign coders to subjects, meaning that all sub- 2print"); jects were analyzed3}) 4**test("**involved 140 5print");experience, ranging 6**if ( true){** their experience with Dart programming ranged from six months7print"); 8}to two years, including unit test development. 9}) From the selected test cases, we developed forms with one ques- tion per test, where the occurrence of each type of test smell was**Listing 13: Example of an Unknown Test in Dart** verified. The developer reviewed the code and answered whether the test smell was present or not. We used the test2.1.14**Verbose Test (VT)**.When a test has more than 30 lines, provided in Section 2.1.it can make the code harder to understand and may indicate that the test has multiple responsibilities, impacting its maintainability. Detection: A test with more than 30 lines withoutcountingnon 3executable statements and annotations.[https://pub.dev/](https://pub.dev/)

SAST’25,

**3.2 Data** We used DNose to detect test smells. After running output file contained the detected test smells for each test. The au- tomated detection with DNose was instantaneous for the selected dataset. A user-friendly graphical interface makes this process eas- ier.

**3.3 Data** Weused the oracle to calculate the accuracy of DNose in relation to manual analysis. DNose detects all instances of a test smell with its exact location the accuracy of DNose and manual analysis considering a test-level granularity. we can detect the AR test smell; thus, we collected data at the test level to analyze them both manually and automatically. Our goal is to show DNose’s accuracy in pinpointing the location of test smells at the test level. Therefore, we provide the accuracy test level, in terms of precision and recall.

**3.4 DNose** This section presents the results of our empirical study on the accu- racy of DNose. The data for replication purposes are available online [ 42 ]. Table 1 reports recall, and F1-score and manual granularity for detecting test smells.

**Table 1: Confusion** **and f1-core data from the DNose tool.**

**TS** AR CTL DA ET EH IT MN PSF RO SE SF TWD UT VT

**4 Experiment** This section outlines the approach we used to structure our study,by Hugging Face. The second step of our experiment was to clone including the research questions and study design.

**4.1 Research** This investigation is guided by a main research question (RQ): is the state of quality in projects developed in the Dart language? further explore this analysis, the main question was broken down into the following sub-questions:

Collection the tool, the

Analysis

(line, module,method,or class). We compared

For example, when evaluating the test-level granularity,

value at the

and Manual Analysis Comparison

the dataobtainedwithaccuracy, precision, valuesfor detectingtest smellswithDNose analysis.Thiscomparisonconsideredthetest-level

matrixwith precision,accuracy, recall

**Accuracy(%)****Precision(%)****Recall(%)****F1-Score(%)** 100100100100 80808080 100100100100 100100100100 901008089 901008089 100100100100 100100100100 100100100100 100100100100 100100100100 100100100100 100100100100 100100100100

Planning

Questions What To

Virgínio

RQWhat is the distribution of test smells in Dart language projects?1 This questionaims to understandhow test smellsare dis- tributedacross Dart projects, which is essential to determine whether they are concentratedin certain types of projects. This initialanalysisallowsustoidentifygeneralquality patterns in projects developed in this language, providing a broad perspective on the adoption of best practices within the Dart ecosystem. RQWhat is the distribution of test smells in the tests of Dart lan-2 guage projects?Testingis a crucial part of software develop- ment, and analyzingthe distributionof test smells specifi- cally in tests helps assess the quality of the test code. This questionprovides detailed information about areas that need improvement and points to practices that may be compro- mising the effectiveness of tests. RQWhich test smells are the most frequently found?The purpose3 of this question is to identify the most common test smells and prioritize mitigation and refactoring efforts. It also helps the development community understand which problematic practices are most prevalent and which specific aspects of testing should receive the most attention during code writing or review. RQWhat arethemostrelevantco-occurrencesandtheirmoti-4 vations?This questiondeterminesthemostrelevantco- occurrences between test smells and helps understand how they interact and potentially amplify test quality issues. This analysis can reveal underlyingcauses for combinedsmell patternsand provide valuable insights into how coding prac- tices can be improved in an integrated manner.

**4.2 Design** In Figure 2, we present an overview of the processes involved in the experimental study. First, 5,410 open-source projects were se- 45lected from the Hugging Face dataset, all sourced from Pub.dev and stored on GitHub. The dataset includes the following informa- tion: title, title link, likes, pub points, popularity, description, latest version, latest version release date, provided license, beta status, SDK, platform, documentationlink, verified publisher, de- pendencies,and GitHub link. The selection of the dataset was based on the number of stars the projects had.

**5 Results** We usedthedatasetfromHuggingfacewitha listof over5,000 (5,410) projects in the Dart language, which were obtained from the Pub.devrepository, the official site for centralizing libraries written in the Dart language and the Flutter framework. In Figure 3, we describe the process of filtering the projects until the final count. Initially, we had access to the list of projects provided

each repository locally. Out of the total 5,410 projects, we managed to clone 4,154. During this stage, we encountered access issues with some repositories, non-existentname changes, as well as 2 or more projects located in the same repository, among other

4[https://huggingface.co/datasets/deepklarity/top-flutter-packages](https://huggingface.co/datasets/deepklarity/top-flutter-packages) 5[https://pub.dev/](https://pub.dev/)

6problems.The third step was to run the DNoseon all the projects,**6 ANALYSIS**AND DISCUSSION which resulted in 2,532 projects with at least one test smell detected,Statisticalanalysis was conducted on a robust dataset of 4,154 Dart meaningapproximately 60% of the cloned projects had at least oneprojects,extracted from the official Pub.dev repository. This yielded occurrence of a test smell.over 20,000 test files and a remarkable 907,566 occurrences of test smells. In Table 2, we present the statistics obtained from our dataset. This tableincludestheTest SmellName,Average of testsmells found per file, Standard Deviation, Median, Square Mean, Max, Min, Sum, Center, and Squares Sum. Despite the substantial volume and diversity of the dataset, we observed significantvariabilityin the distributionof certain test**Figure 3: Project Filtering Workflow: The initial dataset con-** smells. Notably, Magic Number, DuplicateAssert, and Assertion**sisted of**5,410projects,butonly4,154projectscouldbe Rouletteshowed standard deviations considerably higher than their**cloned, and 2,532 contained test smells.** respective averages.Thisindicatesthatthesesmellsaren’tuni- formly distributed across projects but are instead concentrated in a few highly problematic files.We found 14,917 test files “infected”with test smells, out of a This concentrationcan be attributedto differencesin projecttotal of 20,006 test files from the cloned projects, meaning that 74% maturity levels and a lack of consolidated testing standards withinof the test files in the projects have at least one test smell. the Dart community. Therefore, our data isn’t flawed; rather, it accu- rately reflects the current state of testing practices in this ecosystem.**5.1 Answering**the Research Questions This asymmetry, far from being statistical noise, is a crucial finding. Wesummarized the answers for each RQ as follows:It highlightsareasoflowqualityandpresentsclearopportuni- ties for improvementthrough refactoring,linters, or educationalRQWhat isthedistributionofTest SmellsinDartlanguage1 initiatives aimed at enhancing test maintainability and readability.projects?Wefound a distribution of 60% of test smells Wefound over 907,566 test smells. While this sheer volumetivations?**The most**relevantco-occurrencesof“test and diversity are noteworthy, a closer look reveals significant vari-**smells” are discussed in Section 6.1, focusing on corre-** ability in the distributionof certaintest smells.As presentedin**lations above 80%, revealing significant relationships** Table 2,ourdatasetprovidesstatisticsincludingtheTest Smell**between them.**Forexample,thestrongcorrelation Name, Average occurrences per file, Standard Deviation, Median,**between “Assertion Roulette” and “Duplicate Assert”** Square Mean, Max, Min, Sum, Center, and Squares Sum.**(0.86) indicates that tests with multiple undocumented** The Magic Number test smell stands out as the most prevalent,**assertions often contain redundant checks. Similarly,** with a staggering 353,684 occurrences and a peak of 6,413 instances**“Magic Number” and “Ignored Test” (0.85) suggest that** in asingleproject,suggestingitswidespreadandrecurringna-**the lack of clarity in numeric literals may lead to test** ture inDartprojects.Ouranalysisshowedanaverageof17.67**neglect.**These patterns highlight the need for targeted occurrencesper file, but with a median of zero and a high standard**refactoring to mitigate negative impacts on code qual-** deviationof 105.37. First, the ab- rithmic transformation to the occurrence values. The base-10 loga- sence ofcodingstandardsfortestswithintherelativelyyoungrithm is particularly useful because it reduces the large differences Dart/Flutter ecosystemleads to less formal enforcementof goodin magnitude, compressing the values proportionally while main- testing practices, particularly concerning named constants or reusable taining their relative relationships.This makesthe graphsmore fixtures.Second, the low maturity of many analyzed projects in ourbalanced, allowingsmalleroccurrencestobeclearlyvisualized sample, which includeda broad range of open-sourceinitiatives,withoutbeing “overpowered” by the presence of larger values. Tests such as Empty Test and Ignored more focused ones, which would greatly improve test readability.Test have low median values, indicating that, in most cases, they Assertion Roulettealsoappearsfrequently,with200,403occur-are infrequent but appear in specific projects. rences, indicating a tendency towards poorly structured tests thatSome test smellsreach extremevaluesper test file: Assertion complicate failure identification.Roulettewith 2,611, Duplicate Assert with 3,253, and Magic Number Despitethese challenges, the high frequency oftest smells,with 6,413. These elevatedmetrics,combinedwithPrint Statement Fixture and Sleepy Fixture, indicate issues in the extreme values, reveal patterns of technical debt concentratedinmaintenance anddebuggingprocess,potentiallyimpactingtest specific areasof theecosystem.Thisinsightofferscleartargetsefficiency. for interventionthrough refactoring,linter implementation,andConditionaltest logic and exception handling have lower average educationalinitiatives aimed at improving test maintainability andvalues,which suggests that these smells are less common but should readability.still be monitored. As we can observe in the graph in Figure 4, the occurrence values of the test smells show a large discrepancy, especiallydue to the test smells“AssertionRoulette,” “DuplicateAssert”, and“Magic Number.”This large variation in values makes it difficult to analyze

**6.1 Co-ocurrences** In Figure 5, we can see the co-occurrences between the test smells. In the following list, we describe each co-occurrence tail, along with their motivations. This information can be used to generate prioritization strategies for refactorings.

•**AR with DA - Correlation: 0.82 and DA with AR - Cor-** **relation: 0.86 The correlation** “Assertion Roulette” occurs sertion without description), it is very likely that “Duplicate Asserts” (assertions be present. This indicates that the tests are making repeated assertions about the same functionality, making the test re- dundant and hard to maintain. This combination a lack of clarity in the test design, where assertions are not made efficiently [2, 30, 31]. •**AR with EH - Correlation: 0.83** occurs, there

checking

of 0.82 suggests (a test with more than one as-

that when

will also

with“Exception

Handling” high correlation (0.83) indicates that, in addition to using the exception system for making assertions, two anti-patterns are being on error handling to perform their checks languages, the exception handling [22]. •**AR with IT - Correlation: 0.83**

with many assertions are being ignored in Dart projects and not executed. This type of information is concerning, as the motivation for ignoring the test is unclear. It may suggest that the tests are poorly disregarded level [19, 41]. •**AR with SF - Correlation: 0.84**

issues (the use of exception handling in tests). The

This suggests that the tests may(linters) andeducationalinitiativesthatraiseoverallqualityin be poorly structured, with multiple assertions made withoutprojects created with Flutter. clear organization, which can be confusing and difficult to maintain [9, 21].**7 Threats**to Validity •**DA with SF - Correlation: 0.90**The correlation between •**Conclusion Validity** “Duplicate Assert” and “Sleepy Fixture” suggests that tests In the study, the validity of the conclusion may be affected by with multipleredundantassertionsoftenhaveinefficient how the data on test smells were collected and analyzed. ---

<!-- Página 9 -->

On

### also impact the validity of the conclusion. To mitigate this

### threat, a large number of projects from the main repository

### of the Dart language were used.

### •

### Construct Validity

### The definitions of test smells used in the study are based on

### previous works in other languages.

### well-established,

### smells in the Dart language. To mitigate this threat, examples

### of test smells in the Dart language were included.

### •

### External Validity

### The study selected open-source projects from Dart’s official

### repository,

### of all existing Dart projects, especially those that are private

### and corporate.

## 8 Conclusion

### This study investigated the quality of tests in Dart projects, with a

### particular focus on the libraries available in its central repository,

### which are used for developing web, desktop, and mobile applica-

### tions. The analysis,

### alarming presence of test smells in 74% of the test files analyzed,

### totaling 907,566 occurrences.

### portant to note that 60% of the analyzed projects contained at least

### one test smell.

### The results highlight that Magic Number (353,684 occurrences),

### Duplicate Assert

### (200,403 occurrences)

### significant difference compared to the others. This indicates a con-

### cerning trend of poorly

### Dart projects,

### of descriptions for assertions, and the undefined usage of specific

### numbers within the code.

### Additionally,

### smells, as indicated by the standard deviations, suggests that some

### projects concentrate problematic testing practices, neglecting code

### quality.

### The presence of test smells such as Print Statement Fixture

### and Sleepy Fixture raises questions about developers’ knowledge of

### debugging tools, which impacts the maintainability and efficiency

### of tests.

### The co-occurrence

### Number, Duplicate Assert, and Assertion Roulette on overall test

### quality, demonstrating

### smells. The findings of this study provide a crucial perspective on

### the current state of Dart projects and, by extension, the entire Flutter

### framework ecosystem, revealing the need for greater attention to

### test code quality within the community.

### As future work, we intend to conduct a practical study to assess

### the improvements

### coding, aiming to identify test smells before they are committed

### to the production code. Another proposal is to investigate the per-

### sistence of these test smells, analyzing how long it takes for them

### to be

### removed

### duct a detailed study on repositories to analyze, both quantitatively

### and qualitatively, whether

### influence the introduction of test smells.

### on Zenodo: https://zenodo.org/records/14869744.

### available at https://dnose-ts.github.io/, and the DNose source code

### is available on GitHub at https://github.com/tassiovirginio/dnose

### they are

### and a demo is running at https://dnose.onrender.com/ .
